avalancheActivity.comment: The large amounts of fresh snow and drifts blanket a weak old snowpack. Avalanches can easily be triggered by one single winter sports enthusiast in all aspects and grow to large size. Danger zones are numerous, they occur also in the immediate vicinity of the treeline. Activities in outlying terrain are currently dangerous. Remote and natural triggerings can still be expected. Particularly in starting zones which have not yet released, these can grow to very large size and place exposed transportation routes at risk. Rain impact at low and intermediate altitudes is leading to a softening of the snowpack and an increase in naturally triggered avalanche activity.

bulletins.highlights: Naturally triggered avalanches can still be expected

snowpackStructure.comment: By Saturday evening, an additional 20-30cm of fresh snow, locally more, is anticipated. The large amounts of fresh fallen snow are continually being transported in varying directions, new snowdrift accumulations generated, but they are difficult to recognize since constantly getting blanketed with fresh snowfall. Bonding to the unfavorable snowpack is usually weak. Several large and very large avalanche releases from artificial (explosive) triggerings, remote triggerings and triggerings by winter sports enthusiasts confirm just how prone to triggering the snowpack is.

tendency.highlights: The danger of naturally triggered avalanches is diminishing gradually.  Due to increasingly rising temperatures and solar radiation for the first time on the snowpack, the situation for winter sports enthusiasts remains treacherous. As a result of the forecast higher temperatures, wet-snow and glide-snow avalanches will be possible particularly at low and intermediate altitudes.

weatherForecast.comment: A warm front is expected to bring poor visibility and moderate-to-heavy precipitation. Snowfall level will ascend to nearly 1500m. Temperature at 2000m: rising from -5 to -2 degrees; at 3000m: -11 degrees. Strong to storm-strength northwesterly winds at high altitudes.

avalancheActivity.comment: Avalanches can be triggered by one single winter sports enthusiast. Danger zones occur on steep slopes in all aspects, behind drops in the landscape and in wind-loaded gullies and bowls. Frequency and spread of avalanche prone locations increase with ascending altitude. Whumpf noises and fractures which appear when treading upon the snowpack are alarm signals. Activities in outlying terrain demand cautious assessment of the terrain and careful evaluation of the dangers. The inexperienced should not leave secured ski runs. Rain impact is leading to a softening of the snowpack at low and intermediate altitudes; this can lead to naturally triggered, small-to-medium sized avalanches. Zones with glide cracks should be avoided.
avalancheActivity.highlights: Considerable avalanche danger above the timberline

snowpackStructure.comment: By Saturday evening, an additional 10-20cm of fresh snow is anticipated. The amounts of fresh fallen snow are continually being transported in varying directions, new snowdrift accumulations generated, but they are difficult to recognize since constantly getting blanketed with fresh snowfall. Fresh snow and drifts have settled slightly, but bonding to the unfavorable snowpack is usually weak.

tendency.highlights: Particularly at low and intermediate altitudes, increasingly frequent wet-snow and glide-snow avalanches can be expected.

weatherForecast.comment: A warm front is expected to bring poor visibility and moderate-to-heavy precipitation. Snowfall level will ascend to nearly 1500m. Temperature at 2000m: rising from -5 to -2 degrees; at 3000m: -11 degrees. Strong to storm-strength northwesterly winds at high altitudes.
